utility_details.judge_error: No runtime errors detected in either wi_skills or wo_skills runs. Both completed successfully and produced non-empty output files.
utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is more effective and produces higher quality output. Both runs pass all verifier checks and address the core requirements adequately. However, wi_skills demonstrates superior quality in J1.4 (documentation and certification traceability): it explicitly names individual certifying engineers with their Part 66 licence numbers, specifies tech log entry references, and cites EASA Part 145.A.50 CRS requirement with a formal certifying staff table. wo_skills omits the explicit tech log entry content requirement and does not formally designate who issues the Certificate of Release to Service. Additionally, wi_skills provides a more structured, regulatory-grade document with a dispatch decision matrix, formal compliance table for each defect, a risk assessment matrix, and a conditional authorization pathway — all hallmarks of domain-expert engineering judgment. The skill-equipped agent also correctly identifies the 50-minute time window (not 60 minutes as stated by wo_skills) and provides a more precise parallel-activity timeline. Overall, wi_skills produces output that more closely matches the expected regulatory-grade engineering guidance standard.

utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality, technically rigorous structural assessments that passed all verifier checks and satisfied all six judge evaluation criteria. Both outputs correctly identified the crack as the airworthiness-critical element, mandated Part 21 DOA engineering disposition, specified multiple NDT methods with purposes, raised fleet-wide considerations, detailed CRS preconditions, and explicitly notified the operator of grounded status. The wi_skills output (8,143 bytes) is slightly more concise and structurally organized with cleaner tables and an explicit engineering recommendation section. The wo_skills output (10,331 bytes) is more verbose but equally comprehensive, demonstrating that the baseline agent possessed sufficient domain knowledge to produce a regulatory-grade assessment without the skill. In this particular test case, the skill did not produce a meaningfully superior outcome — both outputs are at comparable technical and regulatory quality levels, making the two runs effectively equivalent in terms of task completion.

However, wi_skills clearly outperforms wo_skills on two critical evaluation items: (1) J3.4 fleet-wide systemic risk — wi_skills explicitly raises fatigue-related fleet-wide considerations given the aircraft's 22,400 FC count and prior repair history in the same zone, while wo_skills treats the finding as isolated; (2) J3.6 operator communication — wi_skills explicitly frames the aircraft as grounded with CofA implications and references specific EASA regulatory obligations toward the operator, while wo_skills focuses on internal MRO workflow without adequately addressing formal operator notification. The wi_skills output (22,668 bytes) is also more comprehensive and technically detailed than wo_skills (21,080 bytes), demonstrating deeper aviation structural engineering knowledge consistent with the skill's intended benefit.

items.criterion: The assessment treats the two defects with different airworthiness classifications — the VHF radio defect is identified as an operational/deferrable item while the bleed trip off annunciation is flagged as potentially airworthiness-affecting and requiring more stringent investigation before dispatch. Pass: each defect receives an explicit and distinct classification with a rationale tied to safety impact. Fail: both defects are treated identically, or one is dismissed without airworthiness reasoning.
items.wi_reason: wi_skills clearly differentiates the two defects: VHF-1 is classified as deferrable under MEL 23-11 (does not prevent dispatch), while BLEED TRIP OFF is flagged as 'not dispatchable in its current status' requiring AMM troubleshooting — explicit and distinct classifications with safety rationale.
items.wo_reason: wo_skills also clearly differentiates: VHF-1 assessed as dispatchable under MEL 23-11 (Category B), while BLEED TRIP OFF is assessed as 'not currently dispatchable' pending MEL 36-10 conditions — distinct classifications with explicit rationale for each defect.

items.criterion: The dispatch recommendation for the VHF radio must include the MEL deferral category (e.g., Category B), the applicable time limit (e.g., 3 flight days), and any operational restrictions or crew procedures required. Pass: all three elements (category, time limit, crew/operational restriction) are present for the deferrable defect. Fail: recommendation is a simple yes/no without deferral conditions or is missing any of the three elements.
items.wi_reason: wi_skills references MEL 23-11 for VHF-1 deferral with operational restrictions (crew briefed to use VHF-2/VHF-3 only). However, explicit deferral category (e.g., 'Category B') and time limit (e.g., '3 flight days') are not stated in the output text — missing two of three required elements (category and time limit).
items.wo_reason: wo_skills explicitly states MEL 23-11, deferral Category B, and includes crew operational restriction (use VHF-2 and VHF-3 only). However, the explicit time limit (e.g., 3 flight days) is not directly stated in the output. Category and crew restriction are present; time limit is missing, but category is explicitly stated unlike wi_skills.

Pass: at least one specific maintenance action with an AMM or equivalent reference is listed. Fail: the bleed defect is either rubber-stamped for dispatch or rejected without specifying what investigation determines airworthiness.

items.wo_reason: wo_skills also lists specific AMM references: AMM 36-11-00 fault isolation, AMM 36-11-01 visual inspection, AMM 36-12-00 functional ground check, AMM 45-11-00 CMC interrogation — multiple specific maintenance actions with AMM references clearly stated.

utility_details.judge_error: No runtime errors detected in either wi_skills or wo_skills runs. Note: wo_skills failed the verifier check test_output_contains_dispatch_recommendation (V1.2), likely because the dispatch decision wording ('Aircraft is NOT dispatchable at this time') did not match the verifier's expected pattern, even though a clear dispatch decision is present in the document.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produce high-quality, regulatory-grade dispatch assessments that are remarkably similar in content and structure. Both correctly differentiate the two defects, cite the same MEL item numbers (23-11 and 36-10) and AMM chapter references (36-11-00, 36-11-01, 36-12-00, 45-11-00), specify B1 certifying staff and I1 dual-inspection requirements, and address the turnaround time constraint. The wo_skills output is arguably slightly more explicit on MEL deferral category (explicitly states 'Category B' and 'Category C') and includes the 10-flight-cycle recurrence check requirement. The wi_skills output is slightly more structured with named certifying staff assignments. Overall, both outputs are functionally equivalent and both score 6/6 on the evaluation items. The key difference is that wo_skills failed one verifier check (V1.2) due to phrasing, while wi_skills passed all four. This appears to be a scoring artifact. In terms of substantive quality, both are comparable, with wo_skills being marginally more explicit on MEL categories. The skill did not provide a meaningful advantage in this scenario, as both agents read the same input documents and produced equivalent outputs.

items.criterion: The proposal links specific reliability metrics from the fleet data (e.g., component removal rates, MTBF values) to specific task interval recommendations, and acknowledges data adequacy requirements (sample size, duration) rather than treating any data point as automatically sufficient. items.wo_reason: The wo_skills proposal similarly ties specific reliability metrics (MTBF values, removal rates, failure events) to interval recommendations for at least two tasks, and acknowledges the ATR 30-event sample size threshold as a data adequacy requirement for deferred escalation candidates.

items.criterion: The proposal is structured and worded in a way that addresses what an EASA NAA would need to evaluate the revision — including the regulatory basis for the proposed changes, OEM concurrence requirements, and any required airworthiness authority approvals. items.criterion: The proposal applies different levels of scrutiny and supporting evidence to tasks proposed for escalation versus tasks proposed for interval reduction — reduction candidates driven by elevated failure rates should be treated as higher-urgency items with immediate action framing. utility_details.judge_error: In both runs, the agent reports that the aircraft-maintenance-engineer skill was not found/available ('ABSENT' in wi_skills Step 6, and 'not available' stated in wo_skills Step 4). The wi_skills run explicitly checked for the skill file and found it absent, then proceeded without it. This means neither run actually invoked the skill via /aircraft-maintenance-engineer as instructed. Despite this, both agents produced substantive output from the source documents directly.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills outputs are remarkably similar in quality, scoring 1 on all six evaluation items. Both proposals correctly: apply MSG-3 task type logic differentiating HT/OC/FF tasks; explicitly protect ALI/CMR items; link reliability metrics to recommendations with data adequacy assessment; frame the submission for NAA review with regulatory basis, OEM concurrence, and approval process; differentiate urgency between reduction and escalation candidates; and provide traceable task reference numbers. The similarity is explained by the fact that the aircraft-maintenance-engineer skill was not actually available/invoked in either run — both agents worked from the same source documents with equivalent reasoning. The wi_skills document is marginally more structured (explicit PRESENT/ABSENT skill check, additional regulatory wording refinement step), but the substantive content and quality are effectively equivalent. Neither run demonstrates a clear advantage attributable to the skill.

items.criterion: The assessment correctly identifies that because the crack takes the finding outside SRM limits, an engineering disposition is required from a Part 21 Design Organization (e.g., Airbus Customer Services via the existing technical services agreement), and that this repair cannot be executed under the MRO's Part 145 approval alone. utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced technically rigorous assessments that pass all six judge evaluation items. Both correctly separated the crack from the dent as distinct regulatory findings, identified Part 21 DOA escalation requirements, specified multiple NDT methods with purposes, raised fleet-wide fatigue implications, detailed CRS preconditions, and addressed operator grounding notification. The wi_skills output is marginally more structured with clearer section labeling for the RTS roadmap and slightly more explicit language on the CRS certification authority (Part 66 Cat C), but the wo_skills output is essentially equivalent in regulatory depth and technical accuracy. The quality difference is minimal — both outputs are well above the pass threshold for all items. The skill was not actually invoked in either run (not installed), so the performance gap attributable to the skill itself cannot be directly measured here; both runs relied on the base model's aviation engineering knowledge.

utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality outputs that pass all six evaluation items. However, wi_skills is measurably more effective. The skill-equipped agent produced a longer (20,979 bytes vs 18,533 bytes), more structured document with greater regulatory precision: it cited specific EU regulation numbers (EU 1321/2014, EU 965/2012), provided AMM 36-11 as a specific chapter reference for bleed troubleshooting, included Part M.A.305 record retention requirements, specified dual B1 sign-off requirements for the bleed defect, and provided a more detailed timeline analysis with explicit decision points. The wo_skills agent produced a competent and comprehensive document that also addressed all six criteria, but with slightly less regulatory granularity and less explicit AMM chapter specificity. The difference is one of depth and precision rather than fundamental correctness: wi_skills output reads as regulatory-grade engineering guidance while wo_skills reads as informed professional analysis. wi_skills is the higher quality output.

utility_details.judge_error: No errors detected in either wi_skills or wo_skills runs. Both agents successfully produced the output file and passed all four verifier checks. The wo_skills agent attempted to invoke the skill tool (Step 1 shows skill invocation) but the skill was not available, so it proceeded without skill guidance.
utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is more effective than wo_skills. Both outputs passed all verifier checks and satisfied all six judge evaluation items. However, wi_skills produces a qualitatively superior document: (1) The regulatory framing is more precise, explicitly differentiating between the immediate-action FCU reduction and the routine escalation proposals with stronger urgency language; (2) The OEM concurrence pathway is more clearly specified, requesting NAA endorsement before ATR Engineering Statement of Concurrence; (3) The data adequacy assessment is more thorough, with explicit references to confidence thresholds and sample size requirements; (4) The Annex structures (Annex A showing individual aircraft brake condition data, Annex B revision history) demonstrate a more complete NAA-ready submission package. The wo_skills output, while competent and meeting minimum criteria for all items, reads slightly more like an engineered document and slightly less like an actual regulatory submission, with less emphasis on the procedural pathway from operator proposal to NAA approval to OEM concurrence. The wi_skills output at 34,518 bytes vs. wo_skills at 29,367 bytes also reflects greater depth and completeness.

utility_details.judge_error: No technical errors detected in either run. Both agents completed the task successfully and produced substantial output files (wi_skills: 35,147 bytes / 558 lines; wo_skills: 33,703 bytes / 468 lines). All four verifier checks passed in both runs.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality, technically rigorous structural assessments that passed all six evaluation criteria. Both correctly identified the crack as the airworthiness-critical element outside SRM limits, required Part 21 DOA engineering disposition, specified appropriate NDT methods, raised fleet-wide fatigue considerations, addressed CRS certification requirements, and notified the operator of the grounded status. The wi_skills output is marginally superior in depth and regulatory specificity: it provides more detailed regulatory citations (specific EASA regulation article references), a more comprehensive NDT methodology section (explicitly listing ET, PT, and UT with specific purposes), a more structured CRS roadmap with numbered steps, and a more professionally formatted document with an extensive glossary and formal document control structure. The wo_skills output, while also technically sound and complete against all evaluation criteria, is slightly less detailed in regulatory citations and repair authority hierarchy. The key finding of this evaluation is that the baseline agent (wo_skills) performed surprisingly well on this technically complex scenario, suggesting the underlying LLM already has significant aviation structural engineering knowledge. The skill (wi_skills) provided incremental improvement in document structure, regulatory citation depth, and professional formatting, but did not fundamentally change the regulatory conclusions or technical recommendations.

items.criterion: The return-to-service roadmap specifies the conditions that must be met before a CRS can be issued under EASA Part 145: completed repair per approved engineering data, NDT verification of repair, quality inspection sign-off, and certification by a Part 66 licensed engineer with the appropriate category rating. Pass: at least three of the four CRS preconditions (approved repair data, NDT verification, QA inspection, Part 66 certification) are explicitly addressed. Fail: the roadmap ends at 'complete the repair' without specifying CRS conditions or certification authority.
items.wi_reason: The wi_skills output specifies CRS conditions including: repair per approved engineering data (Airbus SRM or Part 21J DOA), post-repair NDT verification, quality inspection sign-off, and certification by 'authorised Part 145 certifying staff' (Part 66 B1/B2), clearly addressing all four preconditions.
items.wo_reason: The wo_skills output describes a 10-step internal work order sequence including: receive repair disposition, embody repair, perform post-repair inspection/NDT, reinstate configuration, issue CRS and close records. It also references Part 145 CRS issue and approved data requirements, covering at least three of the four preconditions.

items.criterion: The assessment addresses what the aircraft operator must be formally notified of — including that the aircraft is grounded until engineering disposition is received and that the existing CofA does not permit flight with a known crack of this type — rather than framing the situation as a purely internal MRO workflow. Pass: operator notification of grounded status and CofA implications is explicitly addressed. Fail: the output focuses entirely on MRO repair workflow without addressing operator airworthiness obligations or grounding status.
items.wi_reason: The wi_skills output explicitly states 'Aircraft remains unserviceable for release at present' and that the aircraft is 'not eligible for Certificate of Release to Service in its current condition,' clearly communicating grounded status and CofA implications to the operator.
items.wo_reason: The wo_skills output focuses primarily on the internal MRO workflow and maintenance hold, stating the aircraft is 'not airworthy for release,' but does not explicitly address operator notification obligations, formal grounding communication to the operator (Euroflite GmbH), or CofA implications from the operator's perspective. The framing remains largely an internal MRO technical document.

utility_details.judge_error: No runtime errors detected in either wi_skills or wo_skills runs. Both agents completed successfully and produced non-empty output files.
utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills produces a higher quality assessment overall. Both runs pass all verifier checks and address most evaluation criteria competently. The key differentiator is J3.6 (operator communication and airworthiness responsibility framing): the wi_skills output explicitly addresses the operator's grounded status and CofA implications, while the wo_skills output remains focused on internal MRO workflow without formally notifying the operator. Additionally, the wi_skills output is more detailed (16,917 bytes vs 13,299 bytes), provides stronger regulatory citations (Part-145.A.45, Part-145.A.50), and more explicitly frames the crack as categorically outside SRM self-help authority with precise regulatory rationale. The wi_skills output also more clearly distinguishes the dual-path nature of the finding (dent vs. crack) and articulates the DOA escalation trigger with greater precision. While wo_skills is a competent and largely correct assessment, it lacks the operator-facing airworthiness responsibility framing that the skill enables.
